single_use::src/segments/campaign.ts.summary: Single-use (46 LOC): only imported by src/layout.ts

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: layout.ts hardcodes all segment imports — adding a segment requires editing layout.ts

evidence: Lines 4-8 of layout.ts import all 5 segments individually; line 10 assembles them into ALL_SEGMENTS array. Adding a new segment requires both a new file and editing layout.ts imports + array.
detail.suggestion: For a 5-segment codebase this is pragmatic and not worth abstracting. If segment count grows beyond ~8, consider a segments/index.ts barrel that exports the array, keeping the registration concern in the segments directory. Do not over-engineer a dynamic plugin system.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: campaign.ts has dead progress bar logic and contradictory inline comments from iterative development

evidence: Lines 37-48: builds a progress bar (const bar = ...) and assembles a 3-line array (const lines = [line1, bar]; if (line2) lines.push(line2)), but these variables are never used.

Lines 46-48: comments '// Ensure exactly 2 lines: combine bar into line2 area', '// Actually keep 2 lines: line1 + line2 with bar info', '// Simpler: line1 = status + bar, line2 = countdown' show unresolved design iteration.
Lines 49-52: finalLines is built fresh, discarding the progress bar entirely.
detail.suggestion: Remove the dead `bar` computation (lines 37-42), the unused `lines` array (lines 44-45), and the contradictory comments (lines 46-48). Keep only the `finalLines` construction.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: 3 of 5 segments return Block.priority values that differ from their Segment.priority definitions

evidence: modelSegment: Segment.priority=30, but render() returns {priority: 2} on line 93
campaignSegment: Segment.priority=20, but render() returns {priority: 4} on lines 11 and 55
usageSegment: Segment.priority=15, but render() returns {priority: 2} on lines 29 and 52
contextSegment (priority:10) and gitSegment (priority:40) are consistent between Segment and Block
The Block.priority values (2, 4) appear to be from an earlier design and were not updated when Segment priorities were established
detail.suggestion: Use the Segment's priority value in the Block return: e.g. in model.ts line 93, change priority:2 to priority:30. Similarly for campaign.ts and usage.ts. This makes the Block returns consistent with their parent Segment definitions.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: Campaign segment uses id 'promo' while file is campaign.ts and export is campaignSegment

evidence: campaign.ts line 6: id is 'promo'
campaign.ts line 5: export is named campaignSegment
layout.ts line 11: DEFAULT_ORDER references 'promo'

All other segments have consistent naming: context.ts exports contextSegment with id 'context', model.ts exports modelSegment with id 'model', git.ts exports gitSegment with id 'git', usage.ts exports usageSegment with id 'usage'
detail.suggestion: Either rename the file to promo.ts and export promoSegment to match the id, or change the id to 'campaign' and update DEFAULT_ORDER in layout.ts. Since the segment renders promotional rate status and the CLAUDE.md refers to it as 'promo', renaming file/export to promo.ts/promoSegment is the lower-friction path.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Top-level catch in index.ts silently swallows all errors, outputting blank line with no diagnostics

evidence: Line 38: catch {} outputs process.stdout.write('\n') for ANY error — JSON parse failure, render TypeError, or unexpected runtime error all produce identical blank output.
There is no stderr logging, no debug mode, no way for a user to understand why their status line is blank.
This makes debugging impossible: a malformed JSON input from Claude Code, a segment throwing an error, and a missing dependency all look the same.
detail.suggestion: Write a minimal diagnostic to stderr in the catch block: process.stderr.write(`[claude-statusblocks] ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : err}\n`). This preserves the blank-output-on-failure behavior (stdout) while giving developers a diagnostic channel (stderr). Alternatively, write to a log file only when a CLAUDE_STATUSBLOCKS_DEBUG env var is set.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Catch blocks inconsistently document their rationale — some have comments, others are bare

evidence: effort.ts catches have explanatory comments: '/* skip malformed lines */', '/* file not readable */', '/* no settings */'
index.ts inner catches have minimal comments: '/* */' (lines 28, 33)
index.ts outer catch (line 38) has no comment explaining the silent-failure design intent
config.ts catch (line 19) has no comment
git.ts catch (line 26) has no comment
detail.suggestion: Add brief rationale comments to the undocumented catches: index.ts outer catch should explain that blank output is intentional to avoid breaking Claude Code's UI; config.ts should note that missing config means use defaults; git.ts should note that git failures mean the git segment is disabled. Follow effort.ts's example of brief, explanatory catch comments.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::cam.summary: campaignSegment export uses id 'promo' creating name/identity mismatch across codebase

evidence: campaign.ts exports campaignSegment with id: 'promo' (line 6)
layout.ts DEFAULT_ORDER references 'promo' (line 11) but imports campaignSegment from campaign.ts (line 7)
A developer searching for 'campaign' would find the file but miss the config reference; searching for 'promo' would find the config but miss the file
detail.suggestion: Rename file to promo.ts and export as promoSegment to match the id, since 'promo' accurately describes the segment's purpose (promotional rate display). Update the import in layout.ts accordingly.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::res.summary: resetCountdown sounds like an action (reset the countdown) rather than a formatter

evidence: resetCountdown(epochSec: number): string formats seconds-until-reset into a human-readable countdown string like '2h30m'
The 'reset' prefix suggests mutation, but the function is a pure formatter
detail.suggestion: Rename to formatResetCountdown to match the codebase's format* naming convention (formatTokens, formatCountdown in engine.ts) and clarify it is a formatter, not an action.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::res.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: layout.ts render() mixes brute-force layout search, row materialization, and rendering in 120 lines

evidence: Lines 66-90: setup and block rendering (allocWidth, order, active, blocks, widths)
Lines 91-153: brute-force assignment enumeration with reusable buffers (rowOf, seen, rowRW, minIdx), nested loops up to numRows^n iterations
Lines 155-178: materializing bestAssign into RowGroups with sorting and mapping
Lines 180-186: rendering each row group and joining
The function uses 15+ local variables and 4 levels of nesting in the enumeration loop
detail.suggestion: Extract the brute-force layout optimizer (lines 91-153) into a separate function like findOptimalAssignment(n, widths, row1MaxWidth, maxRowWidth) returning the best assignment array. This isolates the combinatorial search from the rendering pipeline and makes both pieces independently understandable.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: model.ts 3-part line2 layout uses 9+ local variables for manual gap distribution

evidence: Lines 58-81: the 3-part branch computes dot, dotLen, eLen, dLen, vLen, contentWidth, spareWithDots, clamped, g, g1, g2, g3, g4, extra, leftGap, rightGap
The inner if/else (lines 67-81) switches between dot-separated and space-separated layouts based on a width threshold of 12
The gap distribution formula (g1..g4 with floor/min/extra arithmetic) is the hardest code to follow in the entire codebase
detail.suggestion: Extract the 3-part gap distribution into a helper function like distributeGaps(parts: string[], targetWidth: number): string that encapsulates the dot-vs-space decision and gap arithmetic. This reduces the render method's cognitive load from 4 branches to 3 simpler ones.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: campaign.ts render builds bar and lines array then discards them, with stale iteration comments

evidence: Line 38-42: builds bar and lines 43-45: builds lines = [line1, bar] with optional line2 push
Lines 46-48: three stale comments showing abandoned design iterations ('Ensure exactly 2 lines', 'Actually keep 2 lines', 'Simpler: line1 = status + bar, line2 = countdown')
Lines 49-52: builds finalLines ignoring both bar and lines variables entirely
detail.suggestion: Remove the bar computation (lines 37-42), the lines array (lines 43-45), and the three stale comments (lines 46-48). Build finalLines directly from line1 and line2.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Layout embeds hardcoded row1 width reduction (maxRowWidth - 5) mixing policy with orchestration

evidence: Line 85: const row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5; is a magic offset for Claude Code's notification panel applied only to the first row, but INK_PADDING (line 12) already accounts for outer padding. The additional 5-char reduction is undocumented and couples layout logic to a specific UI assumption.
detail.suggestion: Extract the row1 extra margin as a named constant (e.g., NOTIFICATION_PANEL_MARGIN = 5) next to INK_PADDING with a comment explaining why row 1 needs additional reduction beyond the general INK_PADDING.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::no_t.summary: No test framework or test files exist anywhere in the codebase

evidence: package.json devDependencies has only typescript and @types/node — no test runner
No test script defined in package.json scripts
No *.test.ts, *.spec.ts, or __tests__/ directories found in repository
detail.suggestion: Add vitest as a devDependency (lightweight, ESM-native, TypeScript out-of-box) and a 'test' script in package.json. Start with unit tests for the highest-value pure-logic modules: colors.ts (visibleLength, padRight, truncate), campaigns/engine.ts (getActiveCampaign with vi.useFakeTimers), and layout.ts (findOptimalAssignment with known block widths).

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::unte.summary: Campaign engine's timezone-aware date arithmetic has zero test coverage

evidence: getActiveCampaign() contains 88 lines of date arithmetic with timezone conversion via Intl.DateTimeFormat, weekend detection, peak/off-peak classification, countdown calculation, and progress computation
getTimeInTz() uses formatToParts which has edge cases (hour=24 normalization on line 27)
Weekend countdown calculates days-until-Monday with manual arithmetic (line 73: daysUntilMon = 8 - dowNum)
Off-peak progress calculation after peak hours involves cross-day arithmetic (line 120: 86400 - peakEndSecs + currentSecs)
detail.suggestion: Write unit tests for getActiveCampaign() using vi.useFakeTimers() to pin Date.now() and verify each state transition: upcoming (before campaign start), active-boosted (off-peak weekday), active-normal (peak hours), weekend, and ended (after campaign end). Test countdown formatting and progress values at boundary times.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::unte.summary: Most-imported utility (colors.ts, 6 importers) has no tests for ANSI-aware string operations

evidence: visibleLength() strips ANSI codes via regex — incorrect stripping would cascade errors to all 5 segments and layout.ts
padRight() delegates to truncate() for over-length strings — ANSI-aware truncation has edge cases (cut point inside escape sequence)
truncate() manually walks the string tracking visible characters and skipping ANSI sequences — off-by-one errors would be invisible without tests
detail.suggestion: Add unit tests for visibleLength (plain text, styled text, nested styles, empty string), padRight (shorter/exact/longer inputs with ANSI), and truncate (cut point inside ANSI sequence, zero width, multi-style strings, string with trailing reset codes).

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::unte.summary: Combinatorial layout row-assignment algorithm has no test coverage

evidence: render() in layout.ts contains a brute-force combinatorial optimizer (numRows^n iterations) that assigns blocks to rows by scoring configurations. This includes edge cases: single block, all blocks on one row, blocks exceeding maxRowWidth forcing multi-row, fallback path when no valid assignment exists. High cyclomatic complexity flagged.
detail.suggestion: Test render() with mock Block inputs at various terminal widths: all blocks fit on one row, forced multi-row, single block, zero-enabled segments. Verify row assignments and output line counts. Mock segment renders to isolate layout logic.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::unte.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: Segment render() returns Block with priority values contradicting segment's declared priority

evidence: modelSegment has priority: 30 on the Segment object but returns { priority: 2 } in the Block (model.ts line 93)
campaignSegment has priority: 20 on the Segment object but returns { priority: 4 } in the Block (campaign.ts line 55)
usageSegment has priority: 15 on the Segment object but returns { priority: 2 } in the Block (usage.ts line 52)
contextSegment and gitSegment are consistent — both use the same priority in the Segment and the returned Block
detail.suggestion: Make each segment's render() return the same priority value as its declared Segment priority. In model.ts change priority: 2 to priority: 30, in campaign.ts change priority: 4 to priority: 20, in usage.ts change priority: 2 to priority: 15. Alternatively, since layout.ts never actually reads Block.priority (it uses the Segment.priority for ordering), consider removing priority from the Block interface entirely to eliminate the inconsistency.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.note: Fixed priority values in model, campaign, usage segments

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually Fixed priority values in model, campaign, usage segments and I am not gaming the score.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: truncate() is exported from colors.ts but has no external consumers

evidence: truncate is called by padRight within colors.ts but no other file imports truncate directly. The export makes it appear as part of the public API surface when it is effectively internal.
detail.suggestion: Remove the export keyword from truncate() to make it a module-private function, reducing the public API surface. If it might be needed externally in the future, keep the export but this is a minor concern.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.note: Removed export from truncate() in colors.ts — only used internally by padRight in the same file

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually Removed export from truncate() in colors.ts — only used internally by padRight in the same file and I am not gaming the score.

review::.::holistic::ai_generated_debt::.summary: Stale thinking-out-loud comments and dead code in campaign.ts from iterative generation

evidence: Lines 46-48 contain three sequential comments showing design iteration: '// Ensure exactly 2 lines: combine bar into line2 area', '// Actually keep 2 lines: line1 + line2 with bar info', '// Simpler: line1 = status + bar, line2 = countdown' — these are working notes, not documentation
The 'bar' variable (line 42-43) and 'lines' array (line 44-45) are computed but never used — the function builds a progress bar then discards it in favor of finalLines, leaving dead intermediate code
detail.suggestion: Remove the three stale comments on lines 46-48. Remove the unused 'bar' variable (lines 42-43) and the unused 'lines' array (lines 44-45). The finalLines array on lines 49-52 is the actual output.

review::.::holistic::ai_generated_debt::.note: Removed dead bar variable, unused lines array, and three stale iteration comments from campaign.ts render()

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually Removed dead bar variable, unused lines array, and three stale iteration comments from campaign.ts render() and I am not gaming the score.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: CONFIG_PATH and SETTINGS_PATH bake homedir() at import time, creating untestable stale snapshots

evidence: config.ts:6 — const CONFIG_PATH = join(homedir(), '.claude-statusblocks.json') computed at module scope
cli.ts:10 — const SETTINGS_PATH = join(homedir(), '.claude', 'settings.json') computed at module scope
contrast with effort.ts:46 which correctly reads homedir() inside the function body: const configDir = process.env.CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR || join(homedir(), '.claude')
detail.suggestion: Move CONFIG_PATH computation inside loadConfig() and SETTINGS_PATH computation inside init(). This follows the pattern already established in effort.ts:46 where homedir() is resolved at call time and allows env var override via CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

resolution_attestation.text: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::c.summary: getActiveCampaign() mixes time arithmetic, state classification, and progress calculation in one 88-line body

evidence: Lines 49-134: getActiveCampaign() handles 5 distinct states (upcoming, weekend, peak, off-peak weekday, no-peak-hours) each with its own time arithmetic, progress calculation, and countdown formatting — these are three distinct responsibilities interleaved
The weekend branch (lines 70-88) computes daysUntilMon, secsUntilPeak, totalWeekendSecs, daysSinceFri, and secsSinceFriPeak inline, mixing temporal calculation with state result construction
detail.suggestion: Extract the time-to-next-state and progress calculations into a helper (e.g., computePeakTiming) that returns {secsUntilChange, progress, isPeak, isWeekend}, then have getActiveCampaign() focus on mapping those results to CampaignStatus objects. This separates the temporal arithmetic from the state classification.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::c.summary: campaign.ts render() builds bar and lines array then immediately discards them for finalLines

evidence: Lines 37-43: builds 'bar' variable with progress bar and 'lines' array [line1, bar], then lines 44-48 contain comments about 'ensure exactly 2 lines' and 'simpler: line1 = status + bar', ultimately replacing everything with 'finalLines' that ignores 'bar' entirely
The 'bar' variable computed on line 42 is never used in the final output — it is dead code from an earlier design iteration
detail.suggestion: Remove the unused 'bar' variable and the 'lines' array (lines 37-48). Build finalLines directly from line1 and line2, eliminating the dead intermediate computation and the confusing commented-out alternative approaches.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: Three segments return Block.priority values that contradict their Segment.priority declarations

evidence: model.ts: Segment.priority = 30 (line 23) but render() returns Block priority: 2 (line 93)
usage.ts: Segment.priority = 15 (line 25) but render() returns Block priority: 2 (line 52)
campaign.ts: Segment.priority = 20 (line 8) but render() returns Block priority: 4 (line 55)
context.ts and git.ts correctly match: both declare and return priority 10 and 40 respectively
detail.suggestion: In each segment's render() return, use the segment's own priority value (30 for model, 15 for usage, 20 for campaign) to match the Segment declaration. Alternatively, if Block.priority is intentionally different from Segment.priority, document why in the Block interface.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.note: Fixed priority values to match Segment declarations

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::camp.summary: campaign.ts render() computes 'bar' progress bar variable that is never included in the output

evidence: Line 42: const bar = color('\u2593'.repeat(filled), barColor) + color('\u2591'.repeat(empty), c.gray) — this progress bar is computed
Line 44: const lines = [line1, bar] — bar is placed in a 'lines' array
Lines 49-52: const finalLines = [line1, line2 || ...] — 'finalLines' completely replaces 'lines', and 'bar' is not referenced
The function returns finalLines (line 55), so 'bar' and 'lines' are dead computations
detail.suggestion: Remove the bar computation (lines 37-43) and the unused 'lines' array (line 44). Also remove the three commented-out alternative approaches (lines 46-48). The function should go directly from building line1/line2 to constructing finalLines.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::unex.summary: layout.ts applies an additional unexplained 5-char offset for row 1 beyond the existing INK_PADDING

evidence: Line 12: const INK_PADDING = 4; // Claude Code's outer paddingX: 2 on each side
Line 68: const maxRowWidth = Math.max(40, termWidth - INK_PADDING); — subtracts 4 for Ink padding
Line 85: const row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5; — subtracts another 5 from row 1 only, with no comment explaining why row 1 needs a tighter width limit than other rows
detail.suggestion: Add a comment explaining why row 1 needs the extra 5-char reduction (likely to avoid overlap with Claude Code's notification panel which only appears at the top). If this is for the notification panel, name the constant (e.g., NOTIFICATION_PANEL_RESERVE = 5).

review::.::holistic::type_safety::unchec.summary: JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData casts external stdin input without runtime validation

evidence: Line 14: const data = JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData — stdin input from Claude Code is cast directly to StatusLineData
If Claude Code's JSON shape changes (e.g., missing context_window or rate_limits fields), the code would crash at property access sites rather than at the parse boundary
The outer try/catch (line 13) does catch and silently writes a newline (line 39), so crashes are handled — but the type assertion makes the type system's promise of structure unreliable
detail.suggestion: Since the top-level try/catch already handles malformed input gracefully, this is low severity. For a more defensive approach, add minimal runtime checks for required fields (e.g., if (!data?.model || !data?.context_window) return) after parsing, rather than adding a full validation library.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::unsafe.summary: Environment variable cast to theme union type without validation

evidence: Line 17: config.theme = envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'] — envTheme is string | undefined, cast to 'default' | 'minimal' | 'full' without checking the value
Contrast with src/effort.ts line 48-49 which correctly validates with includes() before casting: if (['low', 'medium', 'high', 'max'].includes(settings.effortLevel))
detail.suggestion: Add a validation check before the cast, following the pattern already used in effort.ts: const validThemes = ['default', 'minimal', 'full']; if (envTheme && validThemes.includes(envTheme)) config.theme = envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'];

review::.::holistic::type_safety::unsafe.note: not reported in latest holistic re-import

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: layout.ts hardcodes all segment imports — adding a segment requires editing layout.ts

evidence: Lines 4-8 of layout.ts import each segment individually; line 10 manually constructs ALL_SEGMENTS array
Adding a new segment requires: creating the segment file, adding import to layout.ts, adding to ALL_SEGMENTS array, and adding to DEFAULT_ORDER
detail.suggestion: For a 5-segment codebase this is pragmatic and not worth abstracting. If segment count grows beyond ~8, consider a segments/index.ts barrel that exports the array, keeping the registration concern in the segments directory. For now, add a comment noting the manual registration pattern.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: CLI help text lists only 3 of 5 segments, omitting 'promo' and 'usage'

evidence: Lines 69-71 of cli.ts help() list only context, model, git under Blocks section
The actual DEFAULT_ORDER in layout.ts includes 'context', 'model', 'promo', 'git', 'usage' — 5 segments
Users reading help output would not know promo and usage segments exist or can be configured
detail.suggestion: Add the missing segments to the Blocks section in help(): 'promo' (promotional rate status with peak/off-peak countdown) and 'usage' (5-hour and 7-day rate limit bars). Keep the same format as existing entries.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Catch blocks inconsistently document their rationale — some have comments, others are bare

evidence: effort.ts documents all 3 catches: /* skip malformed lines */, /* file not readable */, /* no settings */
index.ts line 28: catch { /* */ } — empty comment placeholder for TTY detection failure
index.ts line 33: catch { /* */ } — empty comment placeholder for tput cols failure
config.ts line 19: catch { — bare, swallows config file read/parse failure
git.ts line 26: catch { return null; } — bare, swallows all git command failures
detail.suggestion: Add brief rationale comments to the undocumented catches: index.ts inner catches should note 'TTY detection fallback' and 'tput fallback'; config.ts should note 'missing or malformed config file — use defaults'; git.ts should note 'not a git repo or git not available'. Follow the pattern already established in effort.ts.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::res.summary: resetCountdown sounds like an action (reset the countdown) rather than a formatter

evidence: resetCountdown (line 4) is a pure formatter: takes an epoch timestamp, returns a human-readable countdown string like '2h30m'
The codebase has established format* as the convention for formatters: formatTokens in context.ts (line 4), formatCountdown in engine.ts (line 41)
The 'reset' prefix suggests an imperative mutation, but the function is side-effect-free
detail.suggestion: Rename to formatResetCountdown to match the codebase's format* naming convention and clarify it is a formatter, not an action.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: model.ts 3-part line2 layout uses 12+ local variables for manual gap distribution

evidence: Lines 58-81: the 3-part layout branches on spareWithDots <= 12 to decide between dot-separated and space-separated layouts
The dot-separated branch (lines 67-75) introduces clamped, g, extra, g1, g2, g3, g4 — seven variables for distributing whitespace evenly across four gaps
A developer reading this must mentally simulate the arithmetic to understand what the output looks like, as the variable names (g1-g4) reveal nothing about which gap they represent
detail.suggestion: Extract the 3-part gap distribution into a helper function like spreadParts(parts: string[], targetWidth: number, separator?: string): string that encapsulates the dot-vs-space decision and gap arithmetic. This reduces model.ts render() to calling spreadParts(parts, line1Width) and makes the layout logic testable in isolation.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: getActiveCampaign() mixes time arithmetic, state classification, and progress calculation in 88 lines

evidence: Lines 49-137: the function handles 5 distinct states (upcoming, ended, weekend, peak, off-peak) with different progress and countdown calculations for each
Weekend progress calculation (lines 72-80) uses 5 intermediate variables (dowNum, daysUntilMon, secsUntilPeak, totalWeekendSecs, daysSinceFri, secsSinceFriPeak) with non-obvious arithmetic
Off-peak weekday progress (lines 116-123) has its own set of intermediate variables for a different calculation, making it hard to see what progress means consistently across states
detail.suggestion: Extract the weekend timing calculation (lines 70-88) into a helper like computeWeekendTiming(currentSecs, peakStartSecs, peakEndSecs, dow) returning {secsUntilChange, progress}. Similarly extract the off-peak weekday timing (lines 104-131). This keeps getActiveCampaign focused on state classification while delegating arithmetic to focused helpers.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Layout embeds hardcoded row1 width reduction (maxRowWidth - 5) mixing policy with orchestration

evidence: Line 171: const row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5 — the magic number 5 represents a notification panel margin but is unnamed and undocumented alongside INK_PADDING which is properly named and commented on line 12
This constant is passed into findOptimalAssignment as a parameter but its rationale is only recoverable by reading Claude Code's UI behavior externally
detail.suggestion: Extract the row1 extra margin as a named constant (e.g., NOTIFICATION_MARGIN = 5) next to INK_PADDING on line 12 with a comment explaining why row 1 needs additional reduction beyond the general INK_PADDING. This keeps the policy declaration together with its sibling constant.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::unte.summary: Combinatorial layout row-assignment algorithm has no test coverage

evidence: findOptimalAssignment() uses brute-force enumeration (numRows^n assignments) with pyramid validation and scoring — complex combinatorial logic with no tests
The algorithm has subtle constraints: row1 gets a different max width, rows are sorted by width ascending, all row indices must be used
materializeAssignment() sorts groups by width — ordering affects visual output
detail.suggestion: Test findOptimalAssignment() with known block widths and verify: single block returns [0], two blocks fitting one row return single-row assignment, blocks forcing two rows produce correct assignment, row1 width constraint causes narrower blocks to land on row 1. Test materializeAssignment() for correct sorting.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: Segment render() allocWidth parameter is only used by 1 of 5 implementations

evidence: Segment interface declares render(data: StatusLineData, availWidth: number): Block
model.ts uses allocWidth to compute dirBudget for directory name truncation (line 34)
context.ts, campaign.ts, git.ts, and usage.ts all ignore the allocWidth parameter entirely — their widths are determined by content
layout.ts computes allocWidth = Math.max(20, maxRowWidth - BOX_CHROME) and passes it to all segments uniformly
detail.suggestion: This is a pragmatic interface design for a 5-segment codebase — making allocWidth optional (availWidth?: number) would add ceremony without value. No code change needed, but if more segments are added, consider whether allocWidth should be part of the interface or passed only to segments that need it via a different mechanism.

review::.::holistic::ai_generated_debt::.summary: cli.ts uses raw ANSI escape strings instead of the project's own color() utility

evidence: cli.ts lines 30, 33-34, 42, 49-51, 61 use raw '\x1b[' escape sequences for coloring, while every other file in the project uses the color()/c.* API from colors.ts
This pattern is consistent with template-pasted code rather than code written with project context
detail.suggestion: Import { color, c } from './colors.js' and replace raw ANSI sequences with color() calls. For example, change console.log('\x1b[1mclaude-statusblocks\x1b[0m preview') to console.log(color('claude-statusblocks', c.bold) + ' preview'). This aligns cli.ts with the project's established color infrastructure.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: CONFIG_PATH and SETTINGS_PATH bake homedir() at import time, creating untestable stale snapshots

evidence: config.ts line 6: const CONFIG_PATH = join(homedir(), '.claude-statusblocks.json') runs at import time
cli.ts line 10: const SETTINGS_PATH = join(homedir(), '.claude', 'settings.json') runs at import time
In contrast, effort.ts line 46 correctly resolves homedir() inside the function body: const configDir = process.env.CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR || join(homedir(), '.claude')
detail.suggestion: Move CONFIG_PATH computation inside loadConfig() in config.ts: replace the module-level const with a local const inside the function body. Similarly, move SETTINGS_PATH computation inside init() in cli.ts. This follows the pattern already established in effort.ts where homedir() is resolved at call time, enabling tests to mock os.homedir() or set environment variables that affect path resolution.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::e.summary: getActiveCampaign() mixes time arithmetic, state classification, and progress calculation in one 88-line body

evidence: Lines 49-137: function handles timezone resolution (line 64), weekend detection and countdown (lines 70-88), peak detection and countdown (lines 90-102), off-peak countdown and progress (lines 104-131), and the no-peak-hours fallback (line 133) — five distinct concerns in one body

The weekend branch (lines 70-88) contains its own progress calculation with totalWeekendSecs/secsSinceFriPeak, while the off-peak branch (lines 116-123) has a separate totalOffPeak/secsSinceLastPeak calculation — these are parallel concerns that could share a structure
detail.suggestion: Extract a computeTimingState(campaign, hour, minute, second, dow) helper that returns {state, secsUntilChange, progress} by encapsulating the peak/off-peak/weekend classification and countdown math. getActiveCampaign() would then focus on campaign selection (date range check) and delegating to the timing helper for the active campaign.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::m.summary: model.ts 3-part line2 layout uses 9+ local variables for manual gap distribution across effort/duration/version

evidence: Lines 58-81: the 3-part branch creates dot, dotLen, eLen, dLen, vLen, contentWidth, spareWithDots, clamped, g, extra, g1, g2, g3, g4, remaining, leftGap, rightGap — 17 local variables for spacing three strings across a width
The two sub-branches (dots vs plain spacing) each construct line2 differently, mixing visual formatting decisions with arithmetic
detail.suggestion: Extract a function like spreadParts(parts: string[], targetWidth: number, separator?: string): string that handles distributing N parts across a target width with optional separators. This replaces the manual variable cascade with a reusable, focused layout helper.

review::.::holistic::design_coherence::e.summary: High cyclomatic complexity in engine.ts driven by interleaved state classification and arithmetic

evidence: Mechanical signal: smells detector flagged 1x High cyclomatic complexity (>15 branches)
The complexity stems from nested if/else branches for weekend, peak, off-peak-before-peak, and off-peak-after-peak — each with its own countdown and progress calculations
detail.suggestion: The cyclomatic complexity is a symptom of the mixed responsibilities identified in engine_mixed_responsibilities. Extracting computeTimingState would naturally reduce the branch depth in getActiveCampaign().

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: campaignSegment.enabled() and render() both call getActiveCampaign() independently, risking state divergence

evidence: Line 8: enabled() calls getActiveCampaign() to check !== null
Line 10: render() calls getActiveCampaign() again independently
If the campaign ends between the enabled() call and the render() call (e.g., at midnight boundary), enabled() returns true but render() gets null and returns a zero-width block
detail.suggestion: This is low-severity because the render() null guard (line 11) handles the case safely. However, the contract would be tighter if campaign state were resolved once. One approach: cache the result in a module-level variable during enabled() and reuse it in render(), similar to gitSegment's cache pattern.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::camp.summary: campaign.ts render() computes barWidth variable that misleadingly suggests a progress bar but is only used for fallback separator sizing

evidence: Line 37: const barWidth = Math.max(visibleLength(line1), visibleLength(line2)) — named 'barWidth' but no bar is rendered
Line 40: barWidth is only used in line2 fallback: line2 || color('─'.repeat(barWidth), c.gray) — it sizes a horizontal rule, not a bar
The name 'barWidth' is a vestige of removed progress bar logic (previously flagged as dead code)
detail.suggestion: Rename barWidth to contentWidth or maxLineWidth to reflect its actual purpose — it computes the wider of the two content lines for separator sizing. This removes the misleading bar reference.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::layo.summary: layout.ts applies an additional unexplained 5-char offset for row 1 beyond the existing INK_PADDING

evidence: Line 12: const INK_PADDING = 4; // Claude Code's outer paddingX: 2 on each side
Line 157: const maxRowWidth = Math.max(40, termWidth - INK_PADDING);
Line 171: const row1MaxWidth = maxRowWidth - 5; — subtracts an additional 5 with only 'extra right margin for notification panel' comment
No documentation explains how 5 was derived or why the notification panel only affects row 1
detail.suggestion: Extract the 5 as a named constant (e.g., NOTIFICATION_OVERLAP = 5) with a comment explaining that Claude Code's notification badge overlaps only the first row's right edge by approximately 5 characters. This makes the relationship between INK_PADDING (general) and NOTIFICATION_OVERLAP (row-1-specific) explicit.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::engi.summary: Weekend progress calculation produces a negative intermediate value before clamping

evidence: Line 79: secsSinceFriPeak = daysSinceFri * 86400 + (currentSecs - peakEndSecs)
On Saturday at 6 AM with peakEndSecs=50400 (14:00): secsSinceFriPeak = 1 * 86400 + (21600 - 50400) = 86400 - 28800 = 57600 — this happens to be positive
But the formula is structurally relying on daysSinceFri * 86400 always dominating the (currentSecs - peakEndSecs) term, which is true but non-obvious
Line 80: Math.max(0, Math.min(1, ...)) provides a safety clamp but the intent of the arithmetic is unclear without it
detail.suggestion: Add a brief comment above line 79 explaining that secsSinceFriPeak measures seconds elapsed since Friday's peak ended, and that the clamp on line 80 handles the edge case where Saturday has not yet reached the peak-end hour. This makes the formula's intent explicit.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::stdin_.summary: JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData casts external stdin input without runtime validation

evidence: Line 14: const data = JSON.parse(input) as StatusLineData — the 'as' cast tells TypeScript to trust that the parsed JSON matches StatusLineData, but there is no runtime check

If upstream sends {model: null} or omits context_window, the code will throw at an arbitrary downstream access point (e.g., data.context_window.used_percentage in context.ts line 15) rather than at the parse boundary
The outer try/catch (line 38) catches these errors but produces a generic error message that does not indicate which field was missing or malformed
detail.suggestion: Add minimal runtime guards after parsing: if (!data?.model?.display_name || !data?.context_window || !data?.workspace?.current_dir) throw new Error('Invalid statusline data: missing required fields'). This validates the most-accessed fields at the boundary without adding a schema validation dependency.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::env_th.summary: Environment variable cast to theme union type without validation

evidence: Line 17: config.theme = envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'] — casts arbitrary string to 'default' | 'minimal' | 'full' without checking
Line 22: same pattern repeated in the catch branch
effort.ts lines 48-49 shows the correct pattern: validates with includes() before casting
detail.suggestion: Add validation before the cast: const validThemes: StatusBlocksConfig['theme'][] = ['default', 'minimal', 'full']; if (envTheme && validThemes.includes(envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme'])) config.theme = envTheme as StatusBlocksConfig['theme']. This follows the pattern already established in effort.ts.
